title: AI Adoption: A Skeptic's Conversion and Future Implications | Brandur Leach posted on the topic | LinkedIn
author: Brandur Leach
text:
Published the latest issue of my newsletter. Like a lot of other newsletters these days, this one is about AI, but unlike most other newsletters, this is a deviation from the norm. I'm a little tired of reading about AI, and suspect I'm not the only one. Next week we'll be going back to regularly scheduled programming. https://lnkd.in/gYdVdpQK I fully admit to previously being an AI skeptic. I told my manager last year that I might be the only person left in the Bay Area who'd never entered anything into an OpenAI prompt. In the last <6 months, I became a convert. Seeing how productive my colleagues were with these tools was the first sign. The second was jumping in myself, and seeing results that floored me. But the real point where I changed my mind was seeing how LLMs could not only generate code, but generate _conventional_ code. I have strong opinions on best practices like strong convention on setup/teardown code in tests, parallelizing everything with `t.Parallel()` annotations, and an entire laundry list of golangci-lint linters. We put these in an `AGENTS.md`, and Claude happily follows every one perfectly, producing code that's review ready almost instantly. These days, I'm a daily, near constant user. My coding productivity is up close to 10x, but my existential worry about the same. In the same day I'll oscillate between thinking that we're all going to be out of the job in two years on one extreme, and on the other, in accordance with Amdahl's Law introducing major efficiencies in generating code/prose is just going to leave bottlenecks elsewhere, so the world isn't going to change that much. The only thing I am sure about is that the coming few years are going to be interesting ones. My generation lived through the invention of the personal computer, the ascendant rise of the internet, the invention of the smartphone, and now we're about to see our four major technical revolution. Special thanks to Mike Fotinakis too for noticing that I'd published this edition and forgotten to send it! (Written by human in accordance with https://brandur.org/human.)
Lol, I still remember the time you were skeptical about the pros of using 'var' in C#, but eventually you embraced it. A few years back, I thought agentic AI coding would need another 5-10 years before it could replace most human coding; it is here already⚡! It is unbelievable how fast AI coding technology evolved and improved. It is sad to see how years of my coding and debugging experience become irrelevant as businesses relentlessly pursue higher productivity and efficiency with AI. After using Claude Sonnet 4.5 and lately, Opus 4.6, it feels impossible to develop software in the "old" fashion way. Maybe in the future, we will include comments like “handcrafted” in PR to demonstrate some level of human creativity 🎨 in programming.
